evidence_spans.supporting_text: here we fabricated three nanoparticle formulations that vary in hydrophobicity, size, and surface charge using nanoprecipitation: KGN-loaded poly(lactic acid-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles (hydrophobic surface, negative charge, ~ 167 nm), PLGA–poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) nanoparticles (hydrophilic surface, positive charge, ~ 297 nm), and PLGA–PEG–hyaluronic acid (HA) nanoparticles (hydrophilic surface, negative charge, ~ 507 nm).
Table 1 Summary of reported kartogenin containing nanoparticle formulations.
Table 2 KGN-loaded nanoparticle properties.
Specifically, we developed KGN-loaded PLGA, PLGA–poly(ethylene glycol) (PLGA–PEG), and PLGA–PEG–hyaluronic acid (PLGA–PEG–HA) nanoparticles.
PLGA was selected as a nanoparticle polymer commonly used in delivery of hydrophobic drugs, including KGN.
HA was selected as a potential targeting moiety for human (hMSCs) through the CD44 cell surface glycoprotein.

evidence_spans.source_locator_text: Fabrication of PLGA, PLGA–PEG, and PLGA–PEG–HA Nanoparticles
evidence_spans.supporting_text: The PLGA–PEG copolymer was synthesized as previously reported. All nanoparticles were prepared using nanoprecipitation.

evidence_spans.source_locator_text: Fabrication of PLGA, PLGA–PEG, and PLGA–PEG–HA Nanoparticles
evidence_spans.supporting_text: PLGA or PLGA–PEG (50 mg) was dissolved in ACN (10 mL) and added to 0.2% w/v PVA in water (100 mL) at 0.5 mL/min. KGN or FITC (5 mg) were included in the polymer solution prior to precipitation for KGN- or FITC-loaded formulations.

evidence_spans.source_locator_text: Fabrication of PLGA, PLGA–PEG, and PLGA–PEG–HA Nanoparticles
evidence_spans.supporting_text: For PLGA–PEG–HA nanoparticles, HA was de-salted by dialyzing against 0.1 M HCl. De-salted HA was solubilized in 0.01 M MES buffer (5 mg/mL, pH 5.5) and activated using EDC and sulfo-NHS (1:5:5 molar ratio) for 2 h at RT under N2. Blank or KGN-loaded PLGA–PEG nanoparticles in MES buffer were added to the activated HA solution (3:1 w/w HA:nanoparticle), and stirred for 24 h at RT under N2.

evidence_spans.source_locator_text: Characterization of Nanoparticle Size and Charge
evidence_spans.supporting_text: Hydrodynamic diameter, polydispersity index (PDI), and ζ potential were measured at 25 °C at a scattering angle of 90° using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, UK).
The encapsulation efficiencies of KGN-loaded PLGA, PLGA–PEG, and PLGA–PEG–HA nanoparticles were ~ 62, 71, and 55%, respectively.

The hydrophobic PLGA particles had the highest drug loading, with approximately 47% of the particle mass attributed to KGN while the hydrophilic formulations exhibited similar KGN loading of approximately 13–16% of particle mass.
An HA content of ~ 36% of particle mass was observed for PLGA–PEG–HA particles.

PLGA and PLGA–PEG–HA nanoparticles exhibited negative ζ potentials, indicative of a negative surface charge, as both HA and PLGA are anionic. PLGA–PEG nanoparticles exhibited positive ζ potential, due to the presence of free amines at the surface.
Functionalizing the PLGA nanoparticle surface with hydrophilic PEG and HA resulted in a significant increase in the hydrodynamic diameter.
Nanoparticle concentrations were selected for each formulation by calculating the amount of nanoparticles necessary to ensure release 100 nM KGN over 3 days based on the in vitro release data.
compared with non-KGN loaded formulations.
all KGN-loaded formulations exhibited a greater sGAG content compared with non KGN-loaded formulations (Fig. 5).

evidence_spans.source_locator_text: Assessing KGN Release and Suspension Stability In Vitro
evidence_spans.supporting_text: To determine KGN release in vitro, KGN-loaded nanoparticle formulations were suspended in PBS (~ 40 mg/mL) and incubated at 37 °C with shaking. Every 3 days for 21 days, the nanoparticle suspensions were centrifuged (14,000 rpm, 10 min) to collect the supernatant, and an equal volume of fresh PBS was added. KGN concentration in the supernatant was quantified using HPLC.

evidence_spans.source_locator_text: Assessing KGN Release and Suspension Stability In Vitro
evidence_spans.supporting_text: The suspension stability of nanoparticle suspensions (1 mg/mL) at these conditions was examined by measuring the hydrodynamic diameter and PDI by DLS.

hMSCs (passage 6) were plated at 10,000 cells/cm2 into a 96-well plate and then treated with growth media only or growth media supplemented with 0.5 mg/mL of nanoparticles with and without KGN loaded. Following a 24-h incubation, CCK-8 solution was added for 2 h at 37 °C and absorbance of the wells was measured at 450 nm using a Cytation3 Plate Reader (BioTek, Winooski, VT). Normalized cell viability was calculated using Eq. (4).

hMSCs (passage 6) were plated at 10,000 cells/cm2 into 6-well plates. Following equilibration in serum-free media for 1 h, FITC-loaded nanoparticles were added to the cells at 0.1 mg/mL and incubated for varying time or incubated for 4 h at varying concentration. Following incubation, the cells were passaged, fixed, and measured using a 488 nm (FITC) laser on a 4-laser, 19-parameter BD FACSAria Illu flow cytometer (Franklin Lakes, NJ).

The sGAG content, indicative of chondrogenesis, was assessed using a Blyscan sGAG assay kit. Briefly, pellets were digested using papain extraction reagent and mixed with Blyscan dye. The dye-sGAG complex was pelleted, dissociated, and solubilized prior to measuring absorbance at 656 nm against chondroitin sulfate standards. The sGAG content was normalized against total wet weight of the pellets. Total DNA was quantified using a PicoGreen assay.
